question: Inside a computer, a transistor's most basic job is to act as a:
correct: Tiny switch that can be turned on or off by an electrical signal
incorrect: Small battery that supplies power to the rest of the circuit
Mechanical gear that turns to move data around the machine
Memory cell that permanently records one character of text
feedback: A transistor is a tiny electronic switch controlled by a voltage. Combining many switches lets a computer represent and process the 1s and 0s of binary.

question: What is a logic gate?
correct: A circuit, built from transistors, that computes a basic operation like AND or OR
incorrect: A physical door that controls airflow to keep the chip cool
A single transistor that stores one bit of data over time
A program instruction that the control unit reads from memory
feedback: Logic gates are small circuits made of transistors. Each gate computes a basic Boolean operation, and gates combine to perform arithmetic.

question: A half-adder is a small circuit that adds two single bits. What two outputs does it produce?
correct: A sum bit and a carry bit
incorrect: A quotient bit and a remainder bit
An input bit and an output bit
A high byte and a low byte
feedback: Adding two bits can produce a result that needs two output bits: the sum and a carry into the next column. The half-adder produces both.

question: Match each logic gate to the output it produces.

pairs.definition: Outputs 1 only when both inputs are 1
Outputs 1 when at least one input is 1
Outputs the opposite of its single input
Outputs 1 only when the two inputs differ

feedback: Each gate implements one Boolean rule. Stacking gates in the right pattern is how a chip performs arithmetic.

question: What is a truth table used for?
correct: Listing the output of a logic gate or circuit for every possible input combination
incorrect: Recording which programs are allowed to run on the machine
Storing the decimal value of each binary number in memory
Tracking how often a cache produces a hit instead of a miss
feedback: A truth table enumerates every input combination and the resulting output, which makes a gate or circuit's behavior fully and unambiguously defined.

question: In the von Neumann architecture, what is the job of the ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit)?
correct: To perform arithmetic and logic operations such as addition and comparison
incorrect: To fetch the next instruction from main memory each cycle
To hold the address of the instruction being executed
To store data permanently after the computer powers off
feedback: The ALU is the part of the CPU that does the actual math and logic. The control unit directs it, but the ALU performs the operations.

question: What is the role of the control unit in a CPU?
correct: To direct the other parts by decoding instructions and coordinating their steps
incorrect: To carry out the addition and subtraction work in a program
To provide the fastest layer of the memory hierarchy
To convert images and sound into binary numbers
feedback: The control unit reads each instruction, decodes what it means, and signals the ALU, registers, and memory to act in the right order.

question: What is a register in a CPU?
correct: A very small, very fast storage location inside the processor itself
incorrect: A large pool of slower storage located outside the processor
A list of the programs that are currently installed on the computer
A circuit that converts decimal numbers into hexadecimal
feedback: Registers are tiny, extremely fast storage cells inside the CPU that hold the values being worked on right now.

question: Put the stages of the fetch–decode–execute cycle in order. Which stage comes first?
correct: Fetch the next instruction from memory
incorrect: Decode the instruction into control signals
Execute the operation in the ALU
Store the final result back into memory
feedback: The CPU first fetches the instruction at the address in the program counter, then decodes it, then executes it, repeating the cycle continuously.

question: Which of the following are parts of a CPU described by the von Neumann architecture? (Select all that apply)
correct: The arithmetic logic unit (ALU)
The control unit
Registers such as the program counter
incorrect: The cooling fan that lowers the chip's temperature
The monitor that displays the program's output
feedback: The ALU, control unit, and registers are core CPU components. A fan and a monitor are useful hardware but are not part of the processor's architecture.

question: The part of the CPU that carries out arithmetic and logic operations is the ___.
correct: ALU
arithmetic logic unit
feedback: The ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit) performs the math and comparisons; the control unit tells it what to do and when.

question: Match each layer of the memory hierarchy to its description.

pairs.definition: Smallest and fastest, located inside the CPU
Small fast layer holding recently used data
Larger and slower, cleared when power is off
Largest and slowest, but keeps data permanently

feedback: The hierarchy trades speed for size: tiny fast layers near the CPU back up large slow layers, so common data stays close and bulk data still fits.

question: What is a cache?
correct: A small, fast layer of memory that keeps recently or frequently used data close to the CPU
incorrect: A backup copy of files saved in case the disk fails later
The permanent storage that survives after the power is off
A list of instructions waiting to be fetched from the disk
feedback: A cache stores copies of data the CPU is likely to need soon, so it can be reached far faster than going all the way to main memory.

question: Caching speeds up real programs because of locality. Which statements about locality are correct? (Select all that apply)
correct: Programs tend to reuse the same data again soon after using it
Programs tend to access memory locations near ones just used
incorrect: Programs access each memory address with the same probability
Programs discard a value right after it has been read once
Caching helps just those programs that happen to avoid loops
feedback: Locality means memory access is predictable: recently used data and nearby data are likely to be used again, which is exactly what a cache exploits.

question: A small, fast layer of memory that holds recently used data close to the CPU is called a ___.
correct: cache
feedback: The cache bridges the speed gap between the very fast CPU registers and slower main memory.

question: A group of 8 bits is called a ___.
correct: byte
feedback: One byte (8 bits) can store a single ASCII character or an unsigned value from 0 to 255.

question: What decimal value does binary {{bits}} represent?
feedback_template: Add the place values of each 1 bit: binary {{bits}} equals {{expected}} in decimal.

question: What decimal value does hexadecimal {{hexValue}} represent?
feedback_template: Hexadecimal is base 16, so {{hexValue}} converts to {{expected}} in decimal.

question: What is the minimum number of bits needed to store the unsigned decimal value {{n}}?
feedback_template: The minimum width equals the length of {{n}} written in binary, which is {{expected}} bits.

question: Which of the following statements about hexadecimal (base 16) are correct? (Select all that apply)
correct: Its digits run from 0 to 9 and then A to F
Each hex digit corresponds to exactly four binary bits
It is often used as a shorter way to write binary values
incorrect: It can represent values that plain binary is unable to show
Each hex digit corresponds to exactly three binary bits
feedback: Hex uses 16 digits (0-9, A-F), and because 16 = 2^4 each digit maps to 4 bits, making hex a compact stand-in for binary. It cannot represent anything binary cannot.

question: Compute {{a}} AND {{b}} on these two bytes. Give the answer in decimal.
feedback_template: Bitwise AND keeps only the bits that are 1 in both numbers, so {{a}} AND {{b}} = {{expected}}.

question: Compute {{a}} OR {{b}} on these two bytes. Give the answer in decimal.
feedback_template: Bitwise OR keeps any bit that is 1 in either number, so {{a}} OR {{b}} = {{expected}}.

question: Compute {{a}} XOR {{b}} on these two bytes. Give the answer in decimal.
feedback_template: Bitwise XOR keeps only the bits that differ between the two numbers, so {{a}} XOR {{b}} = {{expected}}.

question: What problem does two's complement solve?
correct: It gives a way to represent negative integers in binary
incorrect: It lets computers store fractions with a decimal point
It compresses large numbers so they take fewer bits
It converts text characters into binary numbers
feedback: Two's complement is the standard scheme for storing signed integers, so the same addition circuit works for both positive and negative numbers.

question: In an 8-bit two's-complement byte, how do you tell at a glance that the value is negative?
correct: The leftmost (most significant) bit is 1
incorrect: The rightmost (least significant) bit is 1
Each of the eight bits is set to 1
The byte contains an odd number of 1 bits
feedback: In two's complement the high-order bit is the sign bit: 1 means the value is negative, 0 means it is zero or positive.

question: An 8-bit byte holds the bit pattern for unsigned value {{n}}. Read as a two's-complement signed integer, what value does it represent?
feedback_template: Because the high bit is set, the value is negative: subtract 256 from {{n}} to get {{expected}}.

question: Which of the following are true about integer overflow? (Select all that apply)
correct: It happens when a result is too large for the fixed number of bits
It can make a number wrap around to an unexpected value
It can cause real bugs in programs that assume numbers grow without limit
incorrect: It occurs just when a program divides a number by zero
It is impossible on computers that use two's complement
feedback: Fixed-size integers can only hold so many values; exceeding the range wraps around, which has caused real-world bugs. It is unrelated to division by zero and happens regardless of the signed scheme used.

question: What does a character encoding such as ASCII or Unicode define?
correct: Which number stands for each text character
incorrect: How brightly each pixel on the screen should glow
How quickly sound should be sampled per second
How many transistors fit on a single chip
feedback: A character encoding is an agreed-upon map between numbers and characters, so the byte 65 can reliably mean the letter A everywhere.

question: The smallest single dot of color in a digital image is called a ___.
correct: pixel
feedback: An image is a grid of pixels, and each pixel's color is stored as numbers (for example, amounts of red, green, and blue).

question: Match each kind of data to how a computer represents it as numbers.

pairs.definition: A code number assigned to each character
A grid of pixels, each stored as color values
Samples of the wave's height taken over time
A pattern of binary digits in a fixed width

feedback: Different data types use different encodings, but in every case the underlying storage is just numbers in binary.
